***Admiral Scheer*** (German pronunciation: [[atmiˈʁaːl ˈʃeːɐ̯]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) was a [*Deutschland*-class](/wiki/Deutschland-class_cruiser "Deutschland-class cruiser") [heavy cruiser](/wiki/Heavy_cruiser "Heavy cruiser") (often termed a *pocket battleship*) which served with the *[Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine")* (Navy) of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The vessel was named after Admiral [Reinhard Scheer](/wiki/Reinhard_Scheer "Reinhard Scheer"), German commander in the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland"). She was laid down at the *[Reichsmarinewerft](/wiki/Kriegsmarinewerft_Wilhelmshaven "Kriegsmarinewerft Wilhelmshaven")* shipyard in [Wilhelmshaven](/wiki/Wilhelmshaven "Wilhelmshaven") in June 1931 and completed by November 1934. Originally classified as an armored ship (*Panzerschiff*) by the *[Reichsmarine](/wiki/Reichsmarine "Reichsmarine")*, in February 1940 the Germans reclassified the remaining two ships of this class as heavy cruisers.[[a]](#cite_note-grafSpee-2)

The ship was nominally under the 10,160-tonne (10,000-long-ton) limitation on warship size imposed by the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), though with a [full load displacement](/wiki/Full-load_displacement "Full-load displacement") of 15,420 tonnes (15,180 long tons), she significantly exceeded it. Armed with six 28 cm (11 in) guns in two triple [gun turrets](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"), *Admiral Scheer* and her sisters were designed to outgun any cruiser fast enough to catch them. Their top speed of 28 knots (52 km/h; 32 mph) left only a handful of ships in the Anglo-French navies able to catch them and powerful enough to sink them.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPope7-3)

*Admiral Scheer* saw heavy service with the German Navy, including a deployment to Spain during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), where she bombarded the port of [Almería](/wiki/Almer%C3%ADa "Almería"). Her first operation during World War II was a commerce raiding operation into the southern Atlantic Ocean, with a brief foray into the Indian Ocean. During this operation, she sank 113,223 [gross register tons](/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage "Gross register tonnage") (GRT) of shipping, making her the most successful [capital ship](/wiki/Capital_ship "Capital ship") surface raider of the war. After returning to Germany, she was deployed to northern Norway to interdict shipping to the Soviet Union. She was part of an abortive attack on [Convoy PQ 17](/wiki/Convoy_PQ_17 "Convoy PQ 17") and conducted [Operation Wunderland](/wiki/Operation_Wunderland "Operation Wunderland"), a sortie into the [Kara Sea](/wiki/Kara_Sea "Kara Sea"). After returning to Germany at the end of 1942, she served as a training ship until the end of 1944, when she was used to support ground operations against the Soviet Army. She moved to Kiel for repairs in March 1945, where she was capsized by British bombers in a raid on 9 April 1945. She was partially scrapped and the remainder of the wreck was buried when the inner part of Kiel dockyard was filled in after the war.

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design")]

Main article: [*Deutschland*-class cruiser](/wiki/Deutschland-class_cruiser "Deutschland-class cruiser")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Admiral_Scheer_ONI_schematic.jpg/250px-Admiral_Scheer_ONI_schematic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiral_Scheer_ONI_schematic.jpg)

Recognition drawing of *Admiral Scheer* as she appeared in 1942

*Admiral Scheer* was 186 metres (610 ft) [long overall](/wiki/Length_overall "Length overall") and had a [beam](/wiki/Beam_(nautical) "Beam (nautical)") of 21.34 metres (70 ft) and a maximum [draft](/wiki/Draft_(hull) "Draft (hull)") of 7.25 metres (23 ft 9 in). The ship had a design [displacement](/wiki/Displacement_(ship) "Displacement (ship)") of 13,660 metric tons (13,440 long tons) and a [full load](/wiki/Full_load "Full load") displacement of 15,420 tonnes (15,180 long tons),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) though the ship was officially stated to be within the 10,160-tonne (10,000-long-ton) limit of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPope3-5) *Admiral Scheer* was powered by four sets of [MAN](/wiki/MAN_SE "MAN SE") nine-cylinder double-acting two-stroke [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engines "Diesel engines"). The ship's top speed was 28.3 [knots](/wiki/Knot_(unit) "Knot (unit)") (52.4 km/h; 32.6 mph), at 54,000 [PS](/wiki/Metric_horsepower "Metric horsepower") (53,260 shp; 39,720 kW). At a cruising speed of 20 knots (37 km/h; 23 mph), the ship could steam for 9,100 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") (16,900 km; 10,500 mi). As designed, her standard complement consisted of 33 officers and 586 enlisted men, though after 1935 this was significantly increased to 30 officers and 921–1,040 sailors.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4)

*Admiral Scheer*'s [primary armament](/wiki/Main_battery "Main battery") was six [28 cm (11 in) SK C/28](/wiki/28_cm_SK_C/28_naval_gun "28 cm SK C/28 naval gun") guns mounted in two triple [gun turrets](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"), one forward and one aft of the [superstructure](/wiki/Superstructure "Superstructure"). The ship carried a [secondary battery](/wiki/Secondary_armament "Secondary armament") of eight [15 cm (5.9 in) SK C/28](/wiki/15_cm_SK_C/28 "15 cm SK C/28") guns in single turrets grouped [amidships](/wiki/Amidships "Amidships").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) Her heavy anti-aircraft battery consisted of three twin [8.8 cm L/78](/wiki/8.8_cm_SK_C/31_naval_gun "8.8 cm SK C/31 naval gun") guns. These guns were directed by three [SL-4 director posts](/wiki/Stabilisierter_Leitstand "Stabilisierter Leitstand"), installed on each side of the funnel and above the signal bridge. Her middle and light anti-aircraft armament consisted of four twin-mounted [3.7 cm (1.5 in) C/30](/wiki/3.7_cm_SK_C/30 "3.7 cm SK C/30") guns and ten single [2 cm (0.79 in) Flak 30](/wiki/2_cm_Flak_30/38/Flakvierling "2 cm Flak 30/38/Flakvierling") guns. During her refit in 1939-40 the 8.8 cm guns were replaced by three twin [10.5 cm (4.1 in) C/33](/wiki/10.5_cm_FlaK_38 "10.5 cm FlaK 38") guns, two single 2 cm were landed and two quad 2 cm were added. In 1944 her light anti-aircraft armament consisted of five quad and nine single 2 cm guns[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitley_198927-6) By 1945, the anti-aircraft battery had again been reorganized and comprised six 4 cm guns, eight 3.7 cm guns, and thirty-three 2 cm guns.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4)

The ship also carried a pair of quadruple 53.3 cm (21 in) deck-mounted [torpedo tubes](/wiki/Torpedo_tube "Torpedo tube") placed on her stern.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) The ship was equipped with one [Heinkel He 60](/wiki/Heinkel_He_60 "Heinkel He 60") seaplane and one [catapult](/wiki/Aircraft_catapult "Aircraft catapult").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitley_198924,_27-7)[[b]](#cite_note-floatplane-8) *Admiral Scheer*'s [armored belt](/wiki/Belt_armor "Belt armor") was 60 to 80 mm (2.4 to 3.1 in) thick; her upper deck was 17 mm (0.67 in) thick while the main armored deck was 17 to 45 mm (0.67 to 1.77 in) thick. The main battery turrets had 140 mm (5.5 in) thick faces and 80 mm thick sides.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) [Radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") initially consisted of a FMG 39 G(gO) set, though in 1941 this was replaced with an FMG 40 G(gO) set and a FuMO 26 system.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner61-9)[[c]](#cite_note-radar-11)

## Service history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Service history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Admiral_Scheer_at_sea_c._1935.jpg/250px-Admiral_Scheer_at_sea_c._1935.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiral_Scheer_at_sea_c._1935.jpg)

*Admiral Scheer* in 1935

*Admiral Scheer* was ordered by the *Reichsmarine* from the *[Reichsmarinewerft](/wiki/Kriegsmarinewerft_Wilhelmshaven "Kriegsmarinewerft Wilhelmshaven")* shipyard in [Wilhelmshaven](/wiki/Wilhelmshaven "Wilhelmshaven").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) Naval rearmament was not popular with the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and the [Communists](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") in the German *[Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(Weimar_Republic) "Reichstag (Weimar Republic)")*, so it was not until 1931 that a bill was passed to build a second *Panzerschiff*. The money for *Panzerschiff B*, which was ordered as *Ersatz* [*Lothringen*](/wiki/SMS_Lothringen "SMS Lothringen"), was secured after the Social Democrats abstained to prevent a political crisis.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeier-Welcker_et_al.435-12) Her keel was laid on 25 June 1931,[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESieche1980227-13) under construction number 123.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner60-4) The ship was launched on 1 April 1933; at her launching, she was christened by Marianne Besserer, the daughter of Admiral [Reinhard Scheer](/wiki/Reinhard_Scheer "Reinhard Scheer"), the ship's namesake.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson24-14) She was completed slightly over a year and a half later on 12 November 1934, the day she was commissioned into the German fleet.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner62-15) The old [pre-dreadnought battleship](/wiki/Pre-dreadnought_battleship "Pre-dreadnought battleship") [*Hessen*](/wiki/SMS_Hessen "SMS Hessen") was removed from service and her crew transferred to the newly commissioned *panzerschiff*.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson24-14)

At her commissioning in November 1934, *Admiral Scheer* was placed under the command of *[Kapitän zur See](/wiki/Kapit%C3%A4n_zur_See "Kapitän zur See")* (*KzS*) [Wilhelm Marschall](/wiki/Wilhelm_Marschall "Wilhelm Marschall").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16) The ship spent the remainder of 1934 conducting [sea trials](/wiki/Sea_trial "Sea trial") and training her crew.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitley69-17) In 1935, she had a new [catapult](/wiki/Aircraft_catapult#Interwar_and_World_War_II "Aircraft catapult") and landing sail system to operate her Heinkel HE 60 seaplanes on heavy seas installed.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner61-9) From 1 October 1935 to 26 July 1937 her first officer was [Leopold Bürkner](/wiki/Leopold_B%C3%BCrkner "Leopold Bürkner"), later to become head of foreign intelligence in the [Third Reich](/wiki/Third_Reich "Third Reich").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBürkner,_Leopold:_Bordgemeinschaft_der_Emdenfahrer-18) By October 1935, the ship was ready for her first major cruise, when on 25–28 October she visited [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), returning to Kiel on 8 November. The following summer, she cruised out through the [Skagerrak](/wiki/Skagerrak "Skagerrak") and the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") into the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), before visiting [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") on the return voyage.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitley69-17)

### Spanish Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Spanish Civil War")]

*Admiral Scheer*'s first overseas deployment began in July 1936 when she was sent to Spain to evacuate German civilians caught in the midst of the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). From 8 August 1936 she served together with her sister ship [*Deutschland*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Deutschland "German cruiser Deutschland") on [non-intervention patrols](/wiki/Non-intervention_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Non-intervention in the Spanish Civil War") off the [Republican](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)")-held coast of Spain.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson24-14) She served four tours of duty with the non-intervention patrol through June 1937. Her official objective was to control the influx of war [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") into Spain, though she also recorded Soviet ships carrying supplies to the Republicans and protected ships delivering German weapons to [Nationalist](/wiki/National_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "National faction (Spanish Civil War)") forces.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECiupa20-19) During the deployment to Spain, [Ernst Lindemann](/wiki/Ernst_Lindemann "Ernst Lindemann") served as the ship's first gunnery officer.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrützner91–100-20) After *Deutschland* was attacked on 29 May 1937 by [Spanish Republican Air Force](/wiki/Spanish_Republican_Air_Force "Spanish Republican Air Force") aircraft off [Ibiza](/wiki/Ibiza "Ibiza"), *Admiral Scheer* was ordered to bombard the Republican-held port of [Almería](/wiki/Almer%C3%ADa "Almería") in reprisal.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECiupa20-19) On 31 May 1937, the anniversary of the [Battle of Jutland](/wiki/Battle_of_Jutland "Battle of Jutland"), *Admiral Scheer*, flying the [Imperial War Flag](/wiki/Reichskriegsflagge "Reichskriegsflagge"), arrived off Almería at 07:29 and opened fire on shore batteries, naval installations and ships in the harbor. On 26 June 1937, she was relieved by her sister ship [*Admiral Graf Spee*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Admiral_Graf_Spee "German cruiser Admiral Graf Spee"), allowing her to return to Wilhelmshaven on 1 July. She returned to the Mediterranean between August and October, however.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson24-14) In September 1936 *KzS* [Otto Ciliax](/wiki/Otto_Ciliax "Otto Ciliax") had replaced Marschall as the ship's commanding officer.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: World War II")]

At the outbreak of World War II in September 1939, *Admiral Scheer* remained at anchor in the [Schillig](/wiki/Schillig "Schillig") [roadstead](/wiki/Roadstead "Roadstead") outside Wilhelmshaven, with the heavy cruiser [*Admiral Hipper*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Admiral_Hipper "German cruiser Admiral Hipper"). On 4 September, two groups of five [Bristol Blenheim](/wiki/Bristol_Blenheim "Bristol Blenheim") bombers attacked the ships. The first group surprised the anti-aircraft gunners aboard *Admiral Scheer*, who nevertheless managed to shoot down one of the five Blenheims. One bomb struck the ship's deck and failed to explode, and two detonated in the water near the ship. The remaining bombs also failed to explode.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson71-21) The second group of five Blenheims were confronted by the alerted German defenses, which shot down four of the five bombers. *Admiral Scheer* emerged from the attack undamaged.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson71–72-22) In November 1939, *KzS* [Theodor Krancke](/wiki/Theodor_Krancke "Theodor Krancke") became the ship's commanding officer.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson72-23)

*Admiral Scheer* underwent a refit while her sister ships set out on [commerce raiding](/wiki/Commerce_raiding "Commerce raiding") operations in the Atlantic. Around this time the ship received two of its Arado Ar 196 floatplanes.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson33-24) *Admiral Scheer* was modified during the early months of 1940, including the installation of a new, raked [clipper](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper") bow.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson72-23) The heavy command tower was replaced with a lighter structure, and she was reclassified as a [heavy cruiser](/wiki/Heavy_cruiser "Heavy cruiser").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECiupa20-19) Additional anti-aircraft guns were also installed, along with updated radar equipment.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson72-23) On 19–20 July [RAF](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") bombers attacked *Admiral Scheer* and the battleship [*Tirpitz*](/wiki/German_battleship_Tirpitz "German battleship Tirpitz"), though they failed to score any hits.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer33-25) On 27 July, the ship was pronounced ready for service.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson72-23)

#### Atlantic sortie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Atlantic sortie")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-63-64%2C_Panzerschiff_%22Admiral_Scheer%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-63-64%2C_Panzerschiff_%22Admiral_Scheer%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_DVM_10_Bild-23-63-64,_Panzerschiff_%22Admiral_Scheer%22.jpg)

*Admiral Scheer*

*Admiral Scheer* sailed in October 1940 on her first combat sortie. On the night of 31 October she slipped through the [Denmark Strait](/wiki/Denmark_Strait "Denmark Strait") and broke into the open Atlantic.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShowell66-26) Her *[B-Dienst](/wiki/B-Dienst "B-Dienst")* radio intercept equipment identified the [convoy HX 84](/wiki/Convoy_HX_84 "Convoy HX 84"), sailing from [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_(former_city),_Nova_Scotia "Halifax (former city), Nova Scotia"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"). *Admiral Scheer*'s Arado seaplane located the convoy on 5 November 1940.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson33-24) The [armed merchant cruiser](/wiki/Armed_merchant_cruiser "Armed merchant cruiser") [HMS *Jervis Bay*](/wiki/HMS_Jervis_Bay "HMS Jervis Bay"), the sole escort for the convoy, issued a report of the German raider and attempted to prevent her from attacking the convoy, which was ordered to scatter under cover of a smoke screen.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShowell66-26) *Admiral Scheer*'s first salvo scored hits on *Jervis Bay*, disabling her wireless equipment and steering gear. Shells from her second salvo struck the bridge and killed her commander, [Edward Fegen](/wiki/Edward_Stephen_Fogarty_Fegen "Edward Stephen Fogarty Fegen").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards90-27) *Admiral Scheer* sank *Jervis Bay* within 22 minutes, but the engagement delayed the German ship long enough for most of the convoy to escape. *Admiral Scheer* sank only 5 of the convoy's 37 ships, though a 6th was sunk by the Luftwaffe following the convoy's dispersal.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer48-28)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArnold_Hague_Convoy_Database-29)

On 18 December, *Admiral Scheer* encountered the refrigerator ship *Duquesa*, 8,651 GRT. The ship sent off a distress signal, which the German raider deliberately allowed, to draw British naval forces to the area.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer52-30) Krancke wanted to lure British warships to the area to draw attention away from *Admiral Hipper*, which had just exited the Denmark Strait.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards99-31) The [aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") [HMS *Formidable*](/wiki/HMS_Formidable_(67) "HMS Formidable (67)") and [*Hermes*](/wiki/HMS_Hermes_(95) "HMS Hermes (95)"), the cruisers [*Dorsetshire*](/wiki/HMS_Dorsetshire_(40) "HMS Dorsetshire (40)"), [*Neptune*](/wiki/HMS_Neptune_(20) "HMS Neptune (20)"), and [*Dragon*](/wiki/HMS_Dragon_(D46) "HMS Dragon (D46)"), and the armed merchant cruiser [*Pretoria Castle*](/wiki/HMS_Pretoria_Castle "HMS Pretoria Castle") converged to hunt down the German raider, but she eluded the British.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer52-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/StateLibQld_1_127171_Cedar_Trader_%28ship%29.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_127171_Cedar_Trader_%28ship%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_127171_Cedar_Trader_(ship).jpg)

*Admiral Scheer* captured the Norwegian 8,038 [GRT](/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage "Gross register tonnage") oil tanker *Sandefjord* on 18 January 1941, put a prize crew aboard and used her to send prisoners to [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"). After the war *Sandefjord* was rebuilt as the British [bulk carrier](/wiki/Bulk_carrier "Bulk carrier") *Cedar Trader*, shown here.

Between 26 December and 7 January, *Admiral Scheer* rendezvoused with the supply ships [*Nordmark*](/wiki/RFA_Northmark "RFA Northmark") and *Eurofeld*, the auxiliary cruiser [*Thor*](/wiki/German_auxiliary_cruiser_Thor "German auxiliary cruiser Thor"), and the prizes *Duquesa* and *Storstad*. The raiders transferred some 600 prisoners to *Storstad* while they refueled from *Nordmark* and *Eurofeld*.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer53-32) Between 18 and 20 January *Admiral Scheer* captured three Allied merchant ships totalling 18,738 GRT,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer56-33) including the Norwegian [oil tanker](/wiki/Oil_tanker "Oil tanker") *Sandefjord*. She spent Christmas 1940 at sea in the mid-Atlantic, several hundred miles from [Tristan da Cunha](/wiki/Tristan_da_Cunha "Tristan da Cunha"), before making a foray into the Indian Ocean in February 1941.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson34-34)

On 14 February, *Admiral Scheer* rendezvoused with the auxiliary cruiser [*Atlantis*](/wiki/German_auxiliary_cruiser_Atlantis "German auxiliary cruiser Atlantis") and the supply ship [*Tannenfels*](/wiki/MS_Tannenfels "MS Tannenfels") about 1,000 nmi (1,900 km; 1,200 mi) east of [Madagascar](/wiki/Geography_of_Madagascar "Geography of Madagascar"). The raiders resupplied from *Tannenfels* and exchanged information on Allied merchant traffic in the area, parting company on 17 February. *Admiral Scheer* then steamed to the [Seychelles](/wiki/Seychelles "Seychelles") north of Madagascar, where she found two merchant vessels with her Arado floatplanes. She took the 6,994 GRT oil tanker *British Advocate* as a prize and sank the 2,456 GRT Greek-flagged *Grigorios*. A third ship, the 7,178 GRT *Canadian Cruiser*, managed to send a distress signal before *Admiral Scheer* sank her on 21 February. The raider encountered and sank a fourth ship the following day, the 2,542 GRT Dutch steamer *Rantaupandjang*, though she too was able to send a distress signal before she sank.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer59-35)

The British cruiser [HMS *Glasgow*](/wiki/HMS_Glasgow_(C21) "HMS Glasgow (C21)"), which was patrolling in the area, received both messages from *Admiral Scheer*'s victims. *Glasgow* launched reconnaissance aircraft that spotted *Admiral Scheer* on 22 February. Vice Admiral [Ralph Leatham](/wiki/Ralph_Leatham "Ralph Leatham"), the commander of the [East Indies Station](/wiki/East_Indies_Station "East Indies Station"), deployed the carrier *Hermes* and cruisers [*Capetown*](/wiki/HMS_Capetown "HMS Capetown"), [*Emerald*](/wiki/HMS_Emerald_(D66) "HMS Emerald (D66)"), [*Hawkins*](/wiki/HMS_Hawkins "HMS Hawkins"), [*Shropshire*](/wiki/HMS_Shropshire "HMS Shropshire"), and the Australian [HMAS *Canberra*](/wiki/HMAS_Canberra_(D33) "HMAS Canberra (D33)") to join the hunt. Krancke turned to the south-east to evade his pursuers, reaching the South Atlantic by 3 March. The British, meanwhile, had abandoned the hunt on 25 February when it became clear that *Admiral Scheer* had withdrawn from the area.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer59-35)

*Admiral Scheer* then sailed northwards, breaking through the Denmark Strait on 26–27 March and evading the cruisers [*Fiji*](/wiki/HMS_Fiji "HMS Fiji") and [*Nigeria*](/wiki/HMS_Nigeria "HMS Nigeria"). She reached [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen "Bergen"), Norway on 30 March, where she spent a day in the [Grimstadfjord](/wiki/Grimstadfjord "Grimstadfjord"). A destroyer escort joined the ship for the voyage to Kiel, which they reached on 1 April.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer65-36) In the course of her raiding operation, she had steamed over 46,000 nautical miles (85,000 km) and sunk seventeen merchant ships for a total of 113,223 GRT.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson33-24)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer65-36) She was by far the most successful German [capital ship](/wiki/Capital_ship "Capital ship") [commerce raider](/wiki/Commerce_raider "Commerce raider") of the entire war.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHümmelchen101-37) After returning to Germany, Krancke left the ship and was replaced by *KzS* [Wilhelm Meendsen-Bohlken](/wiki/Wilhelm_Meendsen-Bohlken "Wilhelm Meendsen-Bohlken") in June 1941.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16) The loss of the battleship [*Bismarck*](/wiki/German_battleship_Bismarck "German battleship Bismarck") in May 1941, and more importantly, the Royal Navy's destruction of the German supply ship network in the aftermath of the *Bismarck* operation forced a planned Atlantic raiding operation for *Admiral Scheer* and her sister *Lützow* at the end of 1941 to be abandoned.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Hara_et_al.71-38) From 4 to 8 September, *Admiral Scheer* was briefly moved to [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"). There, on 5 and 8 September, [No. 90 Squadron RAF](/wiki/No._90_Squadron_RAF "No. 90 Squadron RAF"), equipped with [Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress](/wiki/Boeing_B-17_Flying_Fortress "Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress") bombers, mounted a pair of unsuccessful attacks on the ship. On 8 September, the ship left Oslo and returned to [Swinemünde](/wiki/Swinem%C3%BCnde "Swinemünde").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer98-39)

#### Deployment to Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Deployment to Norway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Admiral_Scheer_1942.jpg/250px-Admiral_Scheer_1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiral_Scheer_1942.jpg)

*Admiral Scheer*, photographed from [*Prinz Eugen*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Prinz_Eugen "German cruiser Prinz Eugen") en route to Norway

On 21 February 1942, *Admiral Scheer*, the heavy cruiser [*Prinz Eugen*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Prinz_Eugen "German cruiser Prinz Eugen"), and the destroyers [*Z4 Richard Beitzen*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z4_Richard_Beitzen "German destroyer Z4 Richard Beitzen"), [*Z5 Paul Jakobi*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z5_Paul_Jacobi "German destroyer Z5 Paul Jacobi"), [*Z25*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z25 "German destroyer Z25"), [*Z7 Hermann Schoemann*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z7_Hermann_Schoemann "German destroyer Z7 Hermann Schoemann"), and [*Z14 Friedrich Ihn*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z14_Friedrich_Ihn "German destroyer Z14 Friedrich Ihn") steamed to Norway. After stopping briefly in Grimstadfjord, the ships proceeded on to Trondheim. On 23 February, the British submarine [*Trident*](/wiki/HMS_Trident_(N52) "HMS Trident (N52)") torpedoed *Prinz Eugen*, causing serious damage.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer146-40) The first operation in Norway in which *Admiral Scheer* took part was [Operation Rösselsprung](/wiki/Operation_R%C3%B6sselsprung_(1942) "Operation Rösselsprung (1942)"), in July 1942. On 2 July, the ship sortied as part of the attempt to intercept [Arctic convoy](/wiki/Arctic_convoys_of_World_War_II "Arctic convoys of World War II") [PQ-17](/wiki/Convoy_PQ_17 "Convoy PQ 17").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson35-41) *Admiral Scheer* and *Lützow* formed one group while *Tirpitz* and *Admiral Hipper* composed another. While en route to the rendezvous point, *Lützow* and three destroyers ran aground, forcing the entire group to abandon the operation. *Admiral Scheer* was detached to join *Tirpitz* and *Admiral Hipper* in [Altafjord](/wiki/Altafjord "Altafjord").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards194-42) The British detected the German departure and ordered the convoy to scatter. Aware that surprise had been lost, the Germans broke off the surface attack and turned the destruction of PQ-17 over to the [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") and [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe"). Twenty-four of the convoy's thirty-five transports were sunk.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer175–176-43)

In August 1942, she conducted [Operation Wunderland](/wiki/Operation_Wunderland "Operation Wunderland"), a sortie into the [Kara Sea](/wiki/Kara_Sea "Kara Sea") to interdict Soviet shipping and attack targets of opportunity. The length of the mission and the distances involved precluded a destroyer escort for the operation; three destroyers would escort *Admiral Scheer* until they reached [Novaya Zemlya](/wiki/Novaya_Zemlya "Novaya Zemlya"), at which point they would return to Norway. Two U-boats — [*U-251*](/wiki/German_submarine_U-251 "German submarine U-251") and [*U-456*](/wiki/German_submarine_U-456 "German submarine U-456") — patrolled the [Kara Gate](/wiki/Kara_Strait "Kara Strait") and the [Yugorsky Strait](/wiki/Yugorsky_Strait "Yugorsky Strait"). The Germans originally intended to send *Admiral Scheer* with her sister ship *Lützow*, but since the latter had run aground the previous month, she was unavailable for the operation.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterling_&_Tamelander150-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Wunderland.PNG/250px-Wunderland.PNG)](/wiki/File:Wunderland.PNG)

Map showing the route taken by *Admiral Scheer* during [Operation Wunderland](/wiki/Operation_Wunderland "Operation Wunderland")

The operational plan called for strict radio silence to ensure surprise could be maintained. This required Meendsen-Bohlken to have total tactical and operational control of his ship; shore-based commands would be unable to direct the mission.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterling_&_Tamelander150-44) On 16 August, *Admiral Scheer* and her destroyer escort left [Narvik](/wiki/Narvik "Narvik") on a course to pass to the north of Novaya Zemlya. Upon entering the Kara Sea, she encountered heavy ice; in addition to searching for merchant shipping, the Arado floatplane was used to scout paths through the ice fields.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterling_&_Tamelander151-45) On 25 August, she encountered the Soviet [icebreaker](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker") *[Sibiryakov](/wiki/A._Sibiryakov_(icebreaker) "A. Sibiryakov (icebreaker)")*. *Admiral Scheer* sank the icebreaker, but not before she sent a distress signal.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterling_&_Tamelander151–152-46) The German ship then turned south, and two days later, arrived off the port of [Dikson](/wiki/Dikson_Island "Dikson Island"). *Admiral Scheer* damaged two ships in the port and shelled harbor facilities. Meendsen-Bohlken considered sending a landing party ashore, but firing from Soviet shore batteries convinced him to abandon the plan. After breaking off the bombardment, Meendsen-Bohlken decided to return to Narvik. She reached port on 30 August without having achieved any significant successes.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZetterling_&_Tamelander152-47)

On 23 October *Admiral Scheer*, *Tirpitz* and the destroyers *Z4 Richard Beitzen*, [*Z16 Friedrich Eckoldt*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z16_Friedrich_Eckoldt "German destroyer Z16 Friedrich Eckoldt"), [*Z23*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z23 "German destroyer Z23"), [*Z28*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z28 "German destroyer Z28"), and [*Z29*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z29 "German destroyer Z29") left [Bogen Bay](/wiki/Bogen,_Evenes "Bogen, Evenes") and proceeded to [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim "Trondheim"). There, *Tirpitz* stopped for repairs, while *Admiral Scheer* and *Z28* continued on to Germany.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer206-48) *[Fregattenkapitän](/wiki/Fregattenkapit%C3%A4n "Fregattenkapitän")* [Ernst Gruber](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_Gruber&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ernst Gruber (page does not exist)") served as the ship's acting commander at the end of November.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16) In December 1942, *Admiral Scheer* returned to Wilhelmshaven for major overhaul, where she was attacked and slightly damaged by RAF bombers. Consequently, *Admiral Scheer* moved to the less exposed port of Swinemünde.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson35-41) In February 1943, *KzS* [Richard Rothe-Roth](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rothe-Roth&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Rothe-Roth (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Rothe-Roth "de:Richard Rothe-Roth")] took command of the ship.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16) Until the end of 1944, *Admiral Scheer* was part of the Fleet Training Group.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson36-49)

#### Return to the Baltic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Return to the Baltic")]

*KzS* [Ernst-Ludwig Thienemann](/w/index.php?title=Ernst-Ludwig_Thienemann&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ernst-Ludwig Thienemann (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst-Ludwig_Thienemann "de:Ernst-Ludwig Thienemann")], the ship's final commander, took command of *Admiral Scheer* in April 1944.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson22-16) On 22 November 1944, *Admiral Scheer*, the destroyers *Z25* and [*Z35*](/wiki/German_destroyer_Z35 "German destroyer Z35"), and the 2nd Torpedo Boat Flotilla relieved the cruiser *Prinz Eugen* and several destroyers supporting German forces [fighting the Soviets](/wiki/Moonsund_operation "Moonsund operation") on the Estonian island of [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa") (Ösel) in the Baltic.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer373–374-50) The [Soviet Air Force](/wiki/Soviet_Air_Force "Soviet Air Force") launched several air attacks on the German forces, all of which were successfully repelled by heavy anti-aircraft fire.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer374-51) The ship's Arado floatplane was shot down, however.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson36-49) On the night of 23–24 November, the German naval forces completed the evacuation of the island. In all, 4,694 troops were evacuated from the island.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer374-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Kiel%2C_Royal_Air_Force_Bomber_Command%2C_1942-1945_CL2772.jpg/250px-Kiel%2C_Royal_Air_Force_Bomber_Command%2C_1942-1945_CL2772.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kiel,_Royal_Air_Force_Bomber_Command,_1942-1945_CL2772.jpg)

*Admiral Scheer* capsized in Kiel

In early February 1945, *Admiral Scheer* stood off [Samland](/wiki/Sambia_Peninsula "Sambia Peninsula") with several torpedo boats in support of German forces fighting Soviet advances. On 9 February, the ships began shelling Soviet positions. Between 18 and 24 February, German forces launched a local counterattack; *Admiral Scheer* and the torpedo boats provided artillery support, targeting Soviet positions near [Peyse](/wiki/Peyse "Peyse") and [Gross-Heydekrug](/wiki/Vzmorye "Vzmorye"). The German attack temporarily restored the land connection to [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohwer387-52) The ship's guns were badly worn out by March and in need of repair. On 8 March, *Admiral Scheer* departed the eastern Baltic to have her guns relined in Kiel; she carried 800 civilian refugees and 200 wounded soldiers. An uncleared minefield prevented her from reaching Kiel, and so she unloaded her passengers in Swinemünde. Despite her worn-out gun barrels, the ship then shelled Soviet forces outside [Kolberg](/wiki/Kolberg "Kolberg") until she used up her remaining ammunition.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson36-49)

The ship then loaded refugees and left Swinemünde; she successfully navigated the minefields on the way to Kiel, arriving on 18 March. Her stern turret had its guns replaced at the [Deutsche Werke](/wiki/Deutsche_Werke "Deutsche Werke") shipyard by early April. During the repair process, most of the ship's crew went ashore. On the night of 9 April 1945, a general RAF bombing raid by over 300 aircraft struck the harbor in Kiel.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson36-49) *Admiral Scheer* was hit by bombs and capsized. She was partially broken up for scrap after the end of the war, though part of the hull was left in place and buried with rubble from the attack when the inner harbors were filled in post-war.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDodsonCant165top_image-53) The number of casualties from her loss is unknown.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGröner62-15)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESieche1980228-54)

In 2024, an [industrial archeologist](/wiki/Industrial_archaeology "Industrial archaeology") and a [geophysicist](/wiki/Geophysics "Geophysics"), along with a team of researchers using [reflection seismology](/wiki/Reflection_seismology "Reflection seismology"), examined a parking lot of a navy arsenal in Kiel where they suspected the wreck was buried. Some 70 percent of *Admiral Scheer* was located, including the main guns and superstructure.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerliner_Morgenpost2024-55)
